title: On the converse to Eisenstein's last theorem
speaker: Daniel Litt
abstract: Eisenstein proved, in 1852, that if a function f(z) is algebraic, then its Taylor expansion at a point has coefficients lying in some finitely-generated Z-algebra. I will explain ongoing joint work with Josh Lam which studies the extent to which the converse of this theorem holds. Namely, we conjecture that if f(z) satisfies a (possibly non-linear!) algebraic ODE, non-singular at 0, and its Taylor expansion has coefficients lying in a finitely-generated Z-algebra, then f is algebraic. For linear ODE, we prove this conjecture when f(z) satisfies a Picard-Fuchs equation, with initial conditions the class of an algebraic cycle, and in some other cases. For non-linear ODE, we prove it when f(z) satisfies an "isomonodromy" ODE with "Picard-Fuchs" initial conditions. I'll also discuss some motivic corollaries of these results, and explain their relationship to the Grothendieck-Katz p-curvature conjecture.

proof_stages.label: Algebraicity problem and p-curvature background
proof_stages.summary: The talk begins with the problem of recognizing algebraic solutions of algebraic differential equations, recalls the Grothendieck-Katz p-curvature conjecture for linear equations, and explains why nonlinear equations require a different formulation focused on sporadic algebraic solutions.

proof_stages.label: Eisenstein integrality and a nonsingular converse conjecture
proof_stages.summary: Eisenstein's last theorem is stated as finite-prime denominator control for algebraic power series, counterexamples to its naive converse are analyzed, and a conjectural converse is formulated for nonsingular possibly nonlinear ODEs, equating algebraicity with ordinary and strong integrality of the Taylor expansion.

proof_stages.label: Foliation-and-leaf reformulation
proof_stages.summary: Differential equations are recast as horizontal integrable foliations on smooth families, solutions as formal or algebraic leaves, and the conjecture is restated in this geometric language with Gauss-Manin and nonabelian isomonodromy foliations as the core settings.

proof_stages.label: Linear Gauss-Manin theorems
proof_stages.summary: The speaker states joint results proving the conjecture for Gauss-Manin connections at cycle-class/absolute-Hodge initial conditions and for symmetric powers of genus-one families with arbitrary initial condition, along with related linear cases.

proof_stages.label: Nonabelian isomonodromy theorem and p-curvature reduction
proof_stages.summary: For moduli of flat bundles with the isomonodromy foliation, theorem D proves the same algebraicity/integrality equivalence for Gauss-Manin initial flat bundles, and the broader conjecture for universal-curve moduli is related to the classical p-curvature conjecture.

proof_stages.label: Parallel proof mechanism via Hodge extension and finiteness
proof_stages.summary: The proofs of the linear and nonabelian theorems are compared: an algebraic step extends the relevant Hodge or VHS structure along the formal leaf using crystalline/Frobenius and Higgs-de Rham tools, and an analytic step uses polarization or Deligne finiteness to force algebraicity.

proof_stages.label: Local singular predictions and hypergeometric evidence
proof_stages.summary: In Q&A the conjectural viewpoint is shown to predict that an integral nonalgebraic solution of a regular-singular linear ODE should have infinite local monodromy, a prediction not apparent from p-curvature and verified for hypergeometric functions by known classifications.
